title: Fidelity-Guaranteed Entanglement Routing with Distributed Purification Planning
authors: Anthony Gatti, Anoosha Fayyaz, Prashant Krishnamurthy, Kaushik P. Seshadreesan, Amy Babay
abstract: Many quantum-network applications require end-to-end Bell pairs whose fidelity exceeds a request-specific threshold, but existing entanglement routing algorithms either optimize only throughput without regard for fidelity or enforce fidelity guarantees using centralized controllers with global link-state knowledge. We present Q-GUARD, an online entanglement routing algorithm that enforces per-request fidelity thresholds within a distributed protocol model in which nodes exchange link-state information only with their $k$-hop neighbors. After link outcomes are realized in each slot, Q-GUARD builds per-link purification cost tables from realized Bell pairs, allocates per-hop fidelity targets using a Werner-state equal-split rule, and selects between candidate path segments using a segment-local expected-goodput (EXG) metric that jointly accounts for swap success, purification overhead, and resource availability. We also introduce Q-GUARD-WS, an extension that exploits per-link hardware quality estimates to allocate purification effort non-uniformly across hops. On synthetic 100-node topologies with heterogeneous link fidelity and stochastic BBPSSW purification, Q-GUARD raises the qualified success rate from under 20\% to over 85\% on 4-hop paths and nearly doubles the qualified service radius in Euclidean distance relative to throughput-only and naive-purification baselines, while Q-GUARD-WS provides additional throughput gains under high hardware heterogeneity.
summary: The paper introduces Q-GUARD, a distributed routing algorithm for quantum networks that ensures end-to-end entanglement meets specific fidelity requirements. By using local link-state information and adaptive purification planning, it significantly improves the success rate and service range of quantum communication compared to traditional throughput-only methods.
main_problem: Existing quantum network routing algorithms either ignore fidelity or require centralized global knowledge, leading to high latency and decoherence. There is a need for a distributed, fidelity-aware routing protocol that ensures end-to-end entanglement meets specific thresholds.
main_result: The Q-GUARD algorithm increases the qualified success rate from under 20% to over 85% on 4-hop paths and nearly doubles the effective service radius in Euclidean distance compared to throughput-only baselines.
method: The authors propose Q-GUARD, an online distributed algorithm using a k-hop local link-state model, a Werner-state equal-split rule for fidelity target allocation, and an Expected Goodput (EXG) metric for path segment selection.
model_or_system: A quantum network model consisting of nodes and links capable of entanglement generation, BBPSSW purification, and entanglement swapping, modeled using Werner states and a time-slotted, decentralized k-hop topology.
key_observables: Qualified throughput (eps), qualified success rate, service radius (Euclidean distance), and expected goodput (EXG).
important_parameters: Fidelity threshold (Fth), k-hop communication radius, path width (W), hardware heterogeneity (sigma_eta), and purification rounds (Rmax).
main_assumptions: Assumes the BBPSSW purification protocol, Werner-state noise model, and a time-slotted operation with limited k-hop local information exchange.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the routing pipeline of Q-CAST vs Q-GUARD; Figure 2 shows a path detour example; Figure 3 shows performance metrics including qualified throughput vs fidelity threshold, throughput vs hardware heterogeneity, and throughput vs offered load.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of fidelity-aware routing, presents the Q-GUARD and Q-GUARD-WS algorithms, details the physical model and metrics (EXG), evaluates performance via large-scale simulations on synthetic topologies, and analyzes computational complexity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Q-CAST vs. Q-GUARD routing pipelines. Phases 1–3 are shared. Q-GUARD diverges in Phase 4, adding fidelity- aware recovery planning with per-hop purification targets and an EXG-based segment selection metric. Phase 5 executes purification, swapping, and fidelity qualification.
Fig. 2: Illustrative example of Q-GUARD recovery (Fth = 0.8, L = 5 hops).

Fig. 3: Performance across operating conditions (a) Qualified throughput vs. fidelity threshold. Q-CAST’s raw throughput (green) is independent of Fth. (b) Qualified throughput vs. hardware heterogeneity ση. (c) Qualified throughput vs. number of offered S–D pairs. (d) Qualified S–D pairs served vs. number of offered S–D pairs.
Fig. 4: Probability that an S–D pair receives at least one qualified ebit vs. shortest-path hop count (|V |=100, m=1, Fth=0.75).
Fig. 5: Probability that an S–D pair receives at least one qualified ebit vs. Euclidean S–D distance (|V |=100, m=1, Fth=0.75).
